coredata.dc:title: One-pot green synthesis of flower-liked Au NP@GQDs nanocomposites for surface-enhanced Raman scattering
coredata.dc:creator: Fei, Xixi
Liu, Zhiming
Li, Yi
Yang, Guangcun
Su, Chengkang
Zhong, Huiqing
Zhuang, Zhengfei
Guo, Zhouyi
coredata.dc:description: The Au nanoparticles (Au NPs) with bumpy shapes take the advantages of the stronger localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) and more hot spots, which cause them to be the talented surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) substrate. In this work, we reported a one-pot, rapid and green method to fabricate mutli-branched Au nanoparticle@graphene quantum dots (Au NP@GQDs) core-shell nanocomposites via using GQDs as the reducer without adding any other reagents for the first time. The hybrids with a mean diameter of ca. 47 nm have ultrathin 1-nm-thick GQDs shells which coat the flower-liked Au cores. The core-shell structure could provide a win-win pattern to substantially enhance the SERS signals of the dye molecules owing to the GQD coating for its high affinity to absorb the analytes and the bumpy Au NP-core for strong improvement of the LSPR. The Au NP@GQDs exhibited remarkably high SERS activity on different charged dyes, which revealed its universality on SERS detection. Further application of the nanocomposites for simple, sensitive and lable-free SERS detection of malachite green (MG), one of the illegal additives in aquaculture, was conducted, which revealed a superior SERS signals with the enhancement factor of 1.4 × 104 and the minimum detected concentration was as low as 0.5 nM. Moreover, the fantastic SERS repeatability and good structural stability also demonstrated the potential for SERS application. These properties make the flower-liked Au NP@GQDs a promising SERS substrate to detect the banned dye pollutants in the aquaculture and additive colorants in food industry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Flower-liked Au nanoparticles
Surface-enhanced Raman scattering
Dye molecules

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The SERS activities of Au NP@GQD nanocomposites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) GQDs and the Au NP@GQDs at (b) low magnification as well as (c) high magnification (inset: panorama of the nanoparticles and the red arrows pointed to the shells of the hybrids). (d) UV–vis spectra and (e) Raman spectra of GQDs and the Au NP@GQDs. (f) EDX spectrum of the Au NP@GQDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The TEM images of the as-synthesized Au NP@GQD nanocomposites synthesized with 75 μL (a), 125 μL (b), 200 μL (c), 250 μL (d) and 300 μL (e) gold precursor, as well as their corresponding size distribution histograms (f–j), respectively.
The SERS spectra of CV (5 μM) induced by Au NP@GQDs at different phases.

SERS spectra of Rh B (a), CV (b), PS (c) and MG (d) induced by spherical Au NPs (II) and flower-liked Au NP@GQDs (III), respectively. Line I showed the normal Raman spectra of Rh B, CV, PS or MG at the dye concentration of 1 mM.
SERS spectra of MG with different concentrations irritated by Au NP@GQDs (a) and the corresponding quantitative curves (using 1618 cm−1 band, b).

Repeatability of SERS detection of MG absorbed on the same SERS sensor at six different sample spots (insert scheme) (a). Uniformity of SERS intensity at three characteristic Raman peaks according to the SERS detection of the six samples (b).
The stability of the flower-liked Au NPs@GQDs. The SERS spectra of MG in Au NPs@GQDs nanocomposites suspensions (a) and corresponding Raman spectra of the nanocomposites (b) at different times.
Illustration of the preparation of flower-liked Au NP@GQDs and the procedure of SERS experiment.
The enhancement factors (EFs) of the typical SERS peaks in Fig. 4.
Tentative assignment of peaks from SERS spectra of MG molecules on the substrate.
Detection limits of some SERS substrates for MG detection.

head.ce:title: One-pot green synthesis of flower-liked Au NP@GQDs nanocomposites for surface-enhanced Raman scattering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science & SATCM Third Grade Laboratory of Chinese Medicine and Photonics Technology, College of Biophotonics, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510631, Guangdong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science & SATCM Third Grade Laboratory of Chinese Medicine and Photonics Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The two authors contributed equally to this paper.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The Au nanoparticles (Au NPs) with bumpy shapes take the advantages of the stronger localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) and more hot spots, which cause them to be the talented surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) substrate. In this work, we reported a one-pot, rapid and green method to fabricate mutli-branched Au nanoparticle@graphene quantum dots (Au NP@GQDs) core-shell nanocomposites via using GQDs as the reducer without adding any other reagents for the first time. The hybrids with a mean diameter of ca. 47 nm have ultrathin 1-nm-thick GQDs shells which coat the flower-liked Au cores. The core-shell structure could provide a win-win pattern to substantially enhance the SERS signals of the dye molecules owing to the GQD coating for its high affinity to absorb the analytes and the bumpy Au NP-core for strong improvement of the LSPR. The Au NP@GQDs exhibited remarkably high SERS activity on different charged dyes, which revealed its universality on SERS detection. Further application of the nanocomposites for simple, sensitive and lable-free SERS detection of malachite green (MG), one of the illegal additives in aquaculture, was conducted, which revealed a superior SERS signals with the enhancement factor of 1.4 × 104 and the minimum detected concentration was as low as 0.5 nM. Moreover, the fantastic SERS repeatability and good structural stability also demonstrated the potential for SERS application. These properties make the flower-liked Au NP@GQDs a promising SERS substrate to detect the banned dye pollutants in the aquaculture and additive colorants in food industry.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), new kinds of nanophase carbon materials, have attracted a great deal of attention in recent years [1–5]. Composed of single or few layer graphene sheets, GQDs, the closely packed honeycomb structure nano-material, are zero–dimensional nanomaterials with lateral size below 100 nm [1,6,7]. Benefited from its special structure and configuration, GQDs possess the excellent properties in two parts. For the one part, GQDs inherit the characteristics of graphene oxide (GO), such as outstanding water solubility, low cytotoxicity, high surface area and good surface grafting property, etc. [8,9] For the other part, the quantum confinement and edge effect make for high photoluminescence of GQDs [10]. With these extraordinary performance, GQDs are widely used in fluorescence imaging, optoelectronic device, electrochemical catalysis, sensors and drug delivery [5,11–14].

It is worth noting that, more and more studies focused on the GQD-noble metal nanoparticles (NMNPs) hybrids for theirs adhibitions in including imaging, fluorescence sensor and photodynamic therapy [15–17]. More interestingly, the GQD-NMNPs hybrids have been applied as the brilliant SERS substrates based on their better SERS performances compared to the bare NMNPs [7,18,19]. The reasons are as follow: (i) Graphene and their derivatives have the ability to absorb the analyte molecules through π-π stacking and electrostatic interaction [20,21]. (ii) Extra Raman enhancement emerges owing to the charge transfer between the graphene sheet and the molecules [22,23]. Recently, graphene also served as a new type of isolating shell for fabrication of graphene-isolated metal nanoparticles (GIMNs), allowing stable, reproducible and high-performance SERS detection [24–26]. For this, a facile strategy for preparing of metal@graphene core-shell nanostructures has been proposed using GQDs both as the accessible carbon source and as the green reducing agent [27]. The GQDs wrapped metal nanoparticles show remarkable SERS activities, indicating the promising potential for Raman analysis [28,29]. However, the morphologies of current metal@GQD hybrids are often spherical-liked, the metal-cores with multi-tipped shapes have not been reported yet.

Herein, we reported a facile synthesis of Au NP@GQD core-shell nanostructures with flower-like shapes (Scheme 1 ) for the first time. The core-shell nanoparticles are achieved by a one-pot liquid phase reaction using GQDs themselves as the reducing and stabilizing agent without any other chemicals or irradiation. The flower-liked Au NP-core, due to its highly anisotropic geometry, exhibits stronger LSPR which causes a higher EM field located at its tips and leads to more abundant hot spots for generating the more intense Raman signals in comparison to non-branched Au NPs [30,31]. What's more, the flimsy GQDs-shell enables the hybrids to effectively absorb the differently charged dye molecules. On the basis of theirs combined merits, the nanocomposites can serve as an efficient SERS substrate for detection of some organic pollutants. Malachite green (MG), a carcinogenic triarylmethane dye, is often used illegally as the bacteriostat and pesticide in aquaculture. The SERS effect of MG induced by Au NP@GQDs is achieved and shows a low limited detection with wide linear response range.

Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O) was obtained from China National Medicine Corporation (Shanghai, China). Graphene oxide was purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Technology Corporation (Nanjing, China). Crystal violet (CV), Rhodamine B (Rh B), Malachite green (MG) and Ponceau S (PS) were of analytical grade. All reagents were used without further purification.

GQDs were synthesized from GO according to the previous study of Jiang et al. [32] With the details that added 40 mL hydrogen peroxide (30%) and 10 mL ammonia (25–28%) into 5 mL GO solution (4 mg/mL), the mixture was heated to 85 °C for 24 h under drastic stirring. Then, the produced solution was dried in vacuum at 55 °C for about 36 h and the final GQDs solution was obtained by adding ultrapure water to get to the concentration of 4 mg/mL.

0.25 mL GQDs solution (4 mg/mL) was put into 50 mL erlenmeyer flasks with 20 mL ultrapure water. The solution was heated to boiling and kept the reaction condition for 10 min with vigorous stirring. Then, 0.125 mL HAuCl4·4H2O (10 mM) was added into the solution and stirred for 5 min. After the reaction, the above solution would be turned into blue black. It is necessary to centrifuge the aqueous solution at 5000 rmp with 10 min for at least three times to separate the excessive GQDs from flower-liked Au NP@GQDs. Finally, the deposition was resuspended in deionized water, and then, set to the proper concentration. Spherical Au NPs were also synthesized as the control, and the synthesized procedures were as follows: 1 mL HAuCl4·4H2O (10 mM) was diluted with 40.15 mL ultrapure water under sustained stirring in the heated state. At a time when the solution boiled, 0.287 mL sodium citrate aqueous solution (10 mg/mL) was added and boiled for another 15 min. The color of the solution changed from golden yellow to fuchsia, which meant the generation of the spherical Au NPs.

The UV–visible spectra of the nanocomposites were recorded by NanoDrop ND-1000 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) analysis was carried out by a JEM-2100 (HR) transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrum. Raman spectra were collected using Renishaw inVia microspectrometer (Derbyshire, England) under a 785 nm diode laser excitation.

For investigating the SERS effect, the testing sample was prepared by adding 10 μL of 3 mM the flower-liked Au NP@GQDs suspension and 30 μL of the dyestuff aqueous solution (MG, CV, PS or Rh B) into 20 μL ultrapure water and then a droplet of the sample was dropped on a silicon substrate. The SERS measurement was carried out by the Renishaw inVia microspectrometer with a NIR (785 nm) laser excitation and a 20× objective. The laser power on the sample was 6.25 mW with a 15 s accumulation time, which was utilized to obtain the SERS spectra. All experiments were independently conducted at least three times and all of the samples were determined under the same parameters.

The morphologies of the GQDs and flower-liked Au NP@GQDs were investigated by TEM analysis. Fig. 1 a exhibits that the mean size of the GQDs is about 7 nm, which is consistent with the data in the former report [32]. Then the Au NP@GQDs nanocomposites were synthesized using GQDs as the reducer. As seen in Fig. 1b, the low-magnification TEM image illustrates Au NP@GQDs nanocomposites have an irregular urchin-like shape with a mean size of about 47 nm. The Au NP-core wrapped by 1-nm-thick GQDs shell (pointed by the red arrows) is observed (Fig. 1c). The EDX spectrum of Au NP@GQDs exhibits the strong element signals of Au atom, validating the generation of the metal nanostructure (Fig. 1d). The optical properties of GQDs and Au NP@GQDs were characterized by the UV–vis spectrophotometer and Raman spectroscopy. In Fig. 1e, the black curve, standing for the UV–vis spectrum of GQDs, displays a narrow peak at 230 nm which can be attributed to π-π transition of CC bond [10]. As to the absorption spectrum of Au NP@GQDs, there is a LSPR band at 686 nm depending on the varying branch geometries and asymmetric shapes of the flower-liked Au NP-cores [33,34]. Meanwhile, the absorption peak at 230 nm is also presented in the UV–vis spectrum of the nanocomposites, which further proves the existence of GQD coating outside the Au nanostructure. Fig. 1f shows that both the Raman spectra of GQDs (1 mg/mL) and the Au NP@GQDs (1 mg/mL, calculated by the quantity of the GQDs) have two characteristic D and G band. It is no hard to see that the Raman intensities of these two bands of the hybrids are much higher than that of GQDs, which can be ascribed to the linkage between GQDs and Au NPs.

To elaborate the synthesis mechanism, the Au NP@GQDs nanocomposites with various weight ratio between HAuCl4 and GQDs were synthesized via altering the amount of HAuCl4. Fig. 2 demonstrates the morphologies of the Au NP@GQD nanocomposites at different phases investigated by TEM analysis, which reveals an increasing diameter from about 28 nm to 170 nm. In Fig. 2a, most of the phase 1 particles have smooth surfaces with a near-spherical shape, and the sizes are mainly concentrated in 24–32 nm with an average diameter of about 28 nm (Fig. 2f). Compared to phase 1, the nanoparticle at phase 2 presents a dramatically changed shape, which becomes flower-liked, and an obviously increasing particle size of ca. 47 nm in average (Fig. 2b and g). The synthesized mechanism of the branched particles may be similar to the seeding growth approach [30,35], where the larger branched particles are produced through epitaxial deposition of Au atoms on the smaller gold seeds in the present of excess reductant (GQDs). Fig. 2c and h exhibit the incompact structure of phase 3 particles with an average diameter of ca. 146 nm. In comparison, the particles of phase 4 have a smaller size (with an average diameter of ca. 132 nm), tighter structure and less petals. It is probably because more volume of Au ions was added to react for the formation of Au atoms which filled up the void spaces in phase 3 and even resulted in appearance of the a few dendrites on the particle surface. At phase 5 (Fig. 2e), more useless dendrites or fragments appears between the Au particles on account of the excessive gold chloride, leading to disordered nanostructures. Fig. 2j reveals the heterogeneity in the size of phase 5 particles, varying from 100 to 220 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The SERS activities of Au NP@GQD nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: For further exploration of the SERS effect of the Au NP@GQDs nanocomposites, CV (5 μM) was employed as the probe molecule to investigate the SERS activity of different particles (phase 1–5) at the same Au concentration. Fig. 3 clearly shows that the strongest Raman signals of CV is achieved on the phase 2 particles, while the SERS intensities of CV on particles of phase 1 is the weakest. This is because the phase 2 particles shaped like flower (Fig. 1b) can generate a larger electromagnetic filed enhancement and more hot spots, and the GQDs coating has a gift for absorption of the determinants via π-π stacking and electrostatic interaction. The weak SERS signals in the spectrum of phase 1 maybe attributed to the smooth surface and near-spherical shape. In addition, the particles at higher phases also show relatively low SERS activities, which may charge upon their oversized morphologies (3–4 times bigger than that of phase 2) leading to less hot spots.

As pH is a significant factor in SERS study, SERS experiment was conducted by adding of CV into the Au NP@GQDs solutions at different pH values. In Fig. S1, one can see that the optimal SERS effects of the Au NP@GQDs nanocomposites exist at the pH value ranged from 7 to 9.

Next, the optimized nanocomposites were applied as substrates for SERS detection of four different types of dye molecules, such as MG and CV (cationic dyes containing positively charged groups), PS (anionic dye containing negatively charged groups) and Rh B (containing groups with both positive and negative charges). The spherical Au NPs with the size of about 43 nm were used as the control (Fig. S2). Obviously, the SERS signals of all the four dye molecules can be enhanced by both substrates (with the same concentration of 500 μM according to Au ions content) (Fig. 4 ). Compared with spherical Au NPs, the SERS effects of the as synthesized flower-liked Au NP@GQDs are much stronger. To quantitatively evaluate the enhancement ability of flower-liked Au NP@GQDs, the enhancement factor (EF) values of some typical SERS peaks were calculated by employing the following equation:
E F =
I
S E R S
×
C
R a m a n
I
R a m a n
×
C
S E R S

where CSERS stands for the concentration of probe molecules that lead to a SERS signal ISERS, and CRaman represent the concentration of probe molecules that cause a Raman signal IRaman. The data are listed in Table 1 , where the EF values of the Au NP@GQDs are 13.7-, 18.7-, 13.1- and 8.2-fold higher than those of Au NPs. The excellent SERS effects induced by Au NP@GQDs can be explained as follows: On the one hand, the Au NP-core with flower shape can produce a large electromagnetic filed enhancement at its bumped branches than the regular Au nanosphere [36–38]. On the other hand, the GQDs shells of the composites are considered to be affiliative to the aromatic dye molecules due to the π-π stacking interaction and electrostatic interaction. It is rather remarkable that the enhancement of the negative charged PS on spherical Au NPs is extremely weak with an EF value of 70 while the EF value of the nanocomposites is calculated to be 9.2 × 102, which maybe largely ascribed to the π-π stacking interaction between the aromatic molecule and the carbon structure. The excellent SERS effects induced by the Au NP@GQDs, indicating the nanocomposites can be employed for the SERS detection of differently charged dye molecules. Fig. S3 demonstrates the intensity of Raman signals of CV in Au NP@GQDs suspension increased monotonously with the increment of the concentrations of CV, suggesting that SERS effect of the hybrids is in a concentration-dependent manner.

We the further applied the as-prepared Au NP@GQDs nanocomposites to be the SERS substrate for a rapid detection of the MG which is one of the illegal additives in fishery industry for the improvement of fish survival rate with high toxicity. In Fig. 4d, the hybrids are shown to yield an impressive SERS performance and the EF value is estimated to be 1.4 × 104 (1618 cm−1). Some typical SERS peaks at 918 cm−1, 1173 cm−1, 1368 cm−1 and 1618 cm−1 can be attributed to the CH out-of-plane bending, in-plane modes of CH bending, N-phenyl stretching bending, as well as ring CC stretching bending, respectively (Table 2 ) [39,40]. Fig. 5 a shows the SERS spectra of MG with different concentrations in the nanocomposites aqueous solutions. The SERS intensity variation tendency of MG kept in line with that of CV (Fig. S3), as the SERS intensities decline along with the decreasing concentration of MG. The SERS intensity of the peak at 1618 cm−1 vs. the logarithm concentration of MG in a range of 5 × 10−5-5 × 10−10 M was plotted with a great linearity (R2 = 0.973) in Fig. 5b. The experimental limit of detection (LOD) of ca. 0.5 nM (MG) was obtained. Detection limits of different SERS sensors for determination of MG was displayed in Table 3 . Beyond doubt, the SERS activity of the flower-liked Au NP@GQDs nanoparticles come to the top among these substrates. Although the LODs of both Au-coated ZnO nanorods and gallium/Au are at the same order of magnitude with the as-synthesized hybrids, their synthetic methods are either complex or environmentally-unfriendly [41,42]. In general, Au NP@GQDs can be used as a prospective SERS substrate to determine the poison additive in aquaculture or harmful dye molecules with aromatic groups in food industry and hydrographic environment.

The repeatability and stability of Au NP@GQDs were also investigated. Fig. 6 displays the SERS repeatability of the nanocomposites through testing six independent MG samples. It can be discerned the spectral patterns and signal intensities of the SERS spectra are highly unified. In order to describe quantitatively, three typical Raman peaks at 915 cm−1, 1173 cm−1 and 1618 cm−1 are selected which exhibit commendable uniformity since the relative standard deviations (RSD) were calculated as 7.6%, 3.6% and 2.8%, respectively (Fig. 6b). The stability of Au NP@GQDs was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 7 , no obvious changes are observed neither in the SERS spectra of MG induced by the nanohybrids nor in the inherent Raman feature of Au NP@GQDs during 1-month observation, illustrating the good stability of the core-shell nanoparticles (Fig. 7b).

In conclusion, we have reported a one-pot and environment-friendly method to fabricate flower-liked Au NP@GQDs core-shell nanocomposites where GQDs are used as both the reductant and the coating material. The mean diameter of the nanocomposites is ca. 47 nm and the thickness of ultrathin GQDs-shell is about 1 nm. Thanks to the EM and CM enhancement and the high affinity of GQDs to the dye molecules, the nanocomposites can serve as the SERS substrate for sensitive and rapid detection of MG in aqueous solution with the LOD of 0.5 nM. Furthermore, it is also observed that the nanocomposites illustrate the fabulous repeatability and temporal stability, the key parameters of a SERS sensor, which provide the bright prospect of Au NP@GQDs for ultra-sensitive determination of dye contaminants in aquatic products and additives in food industry.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (61335011, 61275187, 21505047 and 61675072), Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education of China (20114407110001 and 20134407120003), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province of China (2014A030310306 and 2014A030311024), the Science and Technology Project of Guangdong Province of China (2012A080203008), the Science and Technology Innovation Project of the Education Department of Guangdong Province of China (2013KJCX0052), and the Scientific Research Cultivation Fund for Young Teachers of South China Normal University (14KJ10).

ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2017.05.072.
